did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Discriminant Analysis (DA) (specifically linear and quadratic DA classifiers) for the classification of skin melanomas and basal cell carcinomas based on combined Raman and autofluorescence spectra.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from an ex vivo study on 79 skin cancer samples (39 melanomas and 40 basal cell carcinomas). It details a specific experimental setup for combined Raman and autofluorescence spectroscopy, describes the data analysis methods (PCA and DA), and reports new diagnostic accuracy metrics (e.g., 97.3% for the combined algorithm). The structure includes standard research sections such as Abstract, Introduction, Materials and Methods, Results, and Discussion.

title: Combined Raman and autofluorescence ex vivo diagnostics of skin cancer in near-infrared and visible regions
brief_summary: This study developed an ex vivo diagnostic method to differentiate between skin melanomas and basal cell carcinomas using combined Raman and autofluorescence spectroscopy. The researchers analyzed 79 histologically confirmed skin cancer samples (39 melanomas and 40 basal cell carcinomas) to extract six spectroscopic criteria related to tissue chromophore alterations. Data processing employed Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Discriminant Analysis (DA) classifiers to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of various spectral criteria combinations. The evaluation demonstrated that a combined 6-criteria multimodal algorithm achieved a classification accuracy of 97.3%, significantly outperforming individual modalities which did not exceed 79% accuracy. The results indicate that integrating Raman and autofluorescence data provides a rapid and reliable tool for malignant skin detection and classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: 39 histologically confirmed melanoma samples were studied. The paper focuses on differentiating melanomas from basal cell carcinomas.
40 histologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma samples were studied. The paper focuses on differentiating basal cell carcinomas from melanomas.

37 normal skin samples were included in the second cohort for comparative analysis of spectral criteria against tumor tissues, though the primary diagnostic classification task focused on distinguishing MM from BCC.

all_task_types.target: Differentiation between skin melanomas (MM) and basal cell carcinomas (BCC), and separation of malignant tissues from normal skin.
all_task_types.details: The study uses Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Discriminant Analysis (DA) (linear and quadratic classifiers) to classify discrete diagnostic categories (MM vs. BCC). The primary task is binary/multiclass classification of cancerous tissue types based on combined Raman and autofluorescence spectra. The paper reports classification accuracy metrics (e.g., 97.3% for the combined 6-criteria algorithm) rather than continuous regression outcomes.

datasets_used.name: First Cohort (RS and NIR AF)
datasets_used.description: A cohort of skin cancer samples undergoing Raman Spectroscopy (RS) and Near-Infrared Autofluorescence (NIR AF) analysis. Samples were collected ex vivo from patients at Samara State Medical University.

use.training: Used for developing and testing the combined 6-criteria multimodal algorithm and individual modality analyses.
use.testing: Used for evaluating diagnostic accuracy (e.g., 97.3% for combined algorithm).

datasets_used.name: Second Cohort (RS and VIS/NIR AF)
datasets_used.description: A cohort of skin tissue samples undergoing Raman Spectroscopy (RS) and Autofluorescence (AF) spectra registration in both visible and near-infrared regions. Samples were collected ex vivo from patients at Samara State Medical University.

use.training: Used for 6-D criteria space analysis (PCA/DA) to separate MM and BCC.
use.testing: Used for evaluating classification accuracy in the 6-D space.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis of spectral criteria against tumor tissues and normal skin.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To reduce the dimensionality of the data while retaining most of the variation in the data set, facilitating the separation of classes in a multidimensional phase space.
To separate two or more classes (melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma) based on different statistical parameters of Gaussian distributions, specifically using linear and quadratic classifiers.
To estimate the correlation between proposed spectroscopic criteria to identify groups of criteria with the lowest correlation for multidimensional space analysis.
To implement the approximation of NIR autofluorescence spectra by minimizing the root-mean-square error.
To implement the approximation of NIR autofluorescence spectra by minimizing the root-mean-square error.
To separate the wide exponentially decaying autofluorescence spectra curve from sharp Raman peaks.
To approximate the NIR autofluorescence spectrum to estimate melanin content based on curvature and slope.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant correlation between the chosen spectroscopic criteria.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant correlation between the chosen spectroscopic criteria.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Significant correlations were identified with a p-value < 0.01 to ensure the independence of the criteria used in the multidimensional classification algorithm.

First Cohort (RS and NIR AF).description: 39 melanomas and 40 basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) analyzed using Raman Spectroscopy (RS) and Near-Infrared Autofluorescence (NIR AF).
First Cohort (RS and NIR AF).notes: The accuracy of cancerous tissues classification reaches 97.3% for a combined 6-criteria multimodal algorithm. The accuracy determined separately for each modality does not exceed 79%. The decrease in accuracy compared to previous studies is attributed to a threefold increase in the number of tested samples.

Second Cohort (RS and VIS/NIR AF).description: 37 normal skins, 27 BCCs, and 10 melanomas analyzed using RS and Autofluorescence (AF) in visible and NIR regions.
Second Cohort (RS and VIS/NIR AF).notes: The combined 6-D method is described as a rapid and reliable tool for malignant skin detection and classification. Analysis of AF spectra only in one spectral range shows 5% to 15% lower values of total accuracy. The lowest accuracy was shown by the combination of VIS AF and RS_1660 criteria.

architecture_used.main_model: Discriminant Analysis (DA) classifiers (linear and quadratic) combined with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction.

architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) used to reduce data dimensionality while retaining variation. Criteria space dimensions included 4-D (for first cohort) and 6-D (for second cohort).
architecture_details.classification_method: Linear and Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (DA) classifiers used to separate classes based on statistical parameters of Gaussian distributions.
architecture_details.criteria_extraction: Six spectroscopic criteria derived from Raman Spectroscopy (RS) and Autofluorescence (AF) spectra: RS_1320, RS_1660, F_I_NIR, F_lambda_NIR, F_I_VIS, F_lambda_VIS. NIR AF spectra approximated by exponential function; VIS AF criteria related to porphyrins, lipids, and flavins content.
architecture_details.optimization_for_criteria: Nelder–Mead simplex method and stochastic method of differential evolution used for approximating NIR autofluorescence spectra by minimizing root-mean-square error. Polynomial approximation used to separate autofluorescence curve from Raman peaks.

training_details.training_method: Supervised classification using linear and quadratic DA on extracted spectroscopic criteria.
training_details.validation_strategy: Ex vivo testing on histologically confirmed samples. No explicit train/validation/test split described; accuracy reported on the full cohort of 79 samples (first cohort) and 74 samples (second cohort).

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Basal Cell Carcinoma) and multiclass separation (Normal Skin vs. Melanoma vs. BCC).
classification_or_output_head.metrics: Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy. Best accuracy of 97.3% achieved with combined 6-criteria multimodal algorithm using linear DA.

preprocessing_techniques: Polynomial approximation to separate the wide exponentially decaying autofluorescence spectra curve from sharp Raman peaks
Additional filtering of random noises during polynomial approximation
Exponential function approximation of NIR autofluorescence spectra to estimate melanin content
Minimization of root-mean-square error for spectral approximation using Nelder–Mead simplex method and stochastic method of differential evolution
Photobleaching of tissue samples by NIR laser irradiation for 2 to 3 minutes before spectra registration to increase Raman signal-to-noise ratio
conclusions: A combined 6-criteria multimodal algorithm using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Discriminant Analysis (DA) achieved a classification accuracy of 97.3% for differentiating skin melanomas from basal cell carcinomas, significantly outperforming individual modalities which did not exceed 79% accuracy.
The combined 6-D method is considered a rapid and reliable tool for malignant skin detection and classification.
Analysis of autofluorescence spectra in only one spectral range (visible or near-infrared) results in 5% to 15% lower total accuracy compared to the combined approach.
The lowest classification accuracy was observed with the combination of visible autofluorescence and the RS1660 Raman criterion.
Using a multidimensional criteria space (4-D or 6-D) improves separation accuracy by 8% to 11% compared to phase plane analysis of single criteria pairs.
The decrease in classification accuracy compared to previous studies is attributed to a threefold increase in the number of tested samples, highlighting the impact of sample variability.

Significant correlations between criteria (p < 0.01) were identified, particularly between Raman criteria (RS1320 and RS1660) due to the contribution of the 1450 cm−1 band, and between Raman and NIR autofluorescence criteria due to melanin influence.
important_remarks: The study is ex vivo, limiting direct clinical applicability without in vivo validation.
No external validation was performed; the dataset was collected from a single center (Samara State Medical University), introducing potential population bias.
The paper does not report a formal train/validation/test split; accuracy metrics are reported on the full cohorts (79 and 74 samples), raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting.
The decrease in classification accuracy compared to previous studies is attributed to a threefold increase in sample size, highlighting sensitivity to sample variability.

Significant correlations (p < 0.01) were found between certain criteria (e.g., RS1320 and RS1660), which may violate the independence assumption of some multivariate methods, although PCA/DA were used to mitigate this.

The study focuses on differentiating melanoma from basal cell carcinoma, with normal skin serving primarily as a comparator in the second cohort rather than a primary diagnostic target in the main classification task.
The lowest accuracy was observed with the combination of visible autofluorescence and the RS1660 Raman criterion, indicating that not all modality combinations are beneficial.
